title: Magic-Informed Quantum Architecture Search
authors: Vincenzo Lipardi, Domenica Dibenedetto, Georgios Stamoulis, Mark H. M. Winands
abstract: Nonstabilizerness, commonly referred to as magic, is a fundamental resource underpinning quantum advantage. In this paper, we propose a magic-informed quantum architecture search (QAS) technique that enables control over a quantum resource within the general framework of circuit design. Inspired by the AlphaGo approach, we tackle the problem with a Monte Carlo Tree Search technique equipped with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) that estimates the magic of candidate quantum circuits. The GNN model induces a magic-based bias that steers the search toward either high- or low-magic regimes, depending on the target objective. We benchmark the proposed magic-informed QAS technique on both the structured ground-state energy problem and on the more general quantum state approximation problem, spanning different sizes and target magic levels. Experimental results show that the proposed technique effectively influences the magic across the search tree and notably also on the resulting final circuit, even in regimes where the GNN operates on out-of-distribution instances. Although introducing a problem-agnostic magic bias could, in principle, constrain the search dynamics, we observe consistent improvements in solution quality across all problems tested.
summary: This paper introduces a new method for designing quantum circuits by using a neural network to predict and control the level of 'magic' or non-classicality during the search process. By biasing the architecture search toward specific magic regimes, the authors achieve better performance in molecular energy estimation and quantum state preparation. This approach provides a way to navigate the complex design space of quantum algorithms more effectively.
main_problem: The difficulty of automatically designing parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) that optimize specific objectives while controlling the level of 'magic' (non-stabilizerness) to target quantum advantage regimes.
main_result: The magic-informed QAS technique effectively biases the search toward desired magic levels, improving solution quality for molecular ground-state energy and quantum state approximation, even when the GNN operates on out-of-distribution circuits.
method: A Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) approach using Progressive Widening (PWMCTS) integrated with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) that estimates the stabilizer 2-Renyi entropy to guide the search.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the design of parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) applied to molecular Hamiltonians (H2, H2O, LiH) and general quantum state approximation tasks.
key_observables: Stabilizer 2-Renlyi entropy (M2), ground-state energy, circuit fidelity, and trace of the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI).
important_parameters: Magic regimes (low, medium, high), circuit depth, number of qubits, and gate sets including CX, H, Rx, Ry, and Rz.
main_assumptions: The GNN provides a computationally efficient but approximate estimation of magic, and the magic-induced bias does not significantly constrain the search for optimal rewards.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows GNN prediction accuracy and Spearman rank correlation for M2; Figure 2 shows the distribution of M2 in random vs. optimized circuits; Figure 7 and 8 show M2 values and QFI matrix traces for state approximation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QAS challenge, proposes the magic-informed MCTS framework using GNNs, evaluates the GNN's ability to estimate magic, benchmarks the method on molecular energy and state approximation tasks, and discusses implications for trainability and scalability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

For each value of the number of qubits n, the plot shows the M2 value distribution of the generated random quantum circuits (grey), of the resulting circuits optimized in M2 using PWMCTS with a number of iterations fixed to 500 (blue), and 2000 (red).
Fig. 1: Graph Neural Network performance. On the left, the performances in terms of M2 estimations, on the right, in terms of Spearman’s ranking correlation.
